meta.title: Where New York came from
meta.subtitle: Foreign-born New Yorkers by country of birth, 1900 to 2020
meta.note: Immigrant waves by country of birth. 1900 and 1910 from the 1910 U.S. Census (Table 29, New York city). 1920 and 1930 from the 1930 U.S. Census (Vol. II, Table 9, New York, N.Y.). 1970-2000 and 2011 from NYC Dept. of City Planning, Newest New Yorkers 2013, Table 2-3 (the 2011 ACS figure is shown in the 2010 column). 2020 from the U.S. Census Bureau American Community Survey 2022 5-year estimates, table B05006, New York city (China combines the mainland, Hong Kong and Taiwan to match the City Planning definition). 1940, 1950 and 1960 are not filled from primary sources, so those cells are blank and the bars interpolate across them (the trend for the big European groups in that span is a steady decline).
caution: Bars show foreign-born New Yorkers only: the immigrant (first) generation, counted by country of birth. US-born children and grandchildren are not in these numbers.

The fade is a visual encoding, not a measured generation count: once a country is past its peak foreign-born decade, its bar dims in proportion to how far it has fallen from that peak. It marks a wave receding into its American-born later generations, whom the census cannot track by nation.

Country borders changed. Poland did not exist as a census birthplace before 1920 (Poles were counted under Russia, Austria and Germany), so its bar enters in 1920. 'Russia / U.S.S.R.' is one continuous bar (Russian Empire to Russia); Ukraine breaks out separately from 2000. The Austria and Hungary counts for 1920 were disrupted by the break-up of Austria-Hungary and are not strictly comparable across that decade. Czechoslovakia enters in 1920 and is dropped after 2010 (it dissolved). 'United Kingdom' uses England-born figures for 1900-1930 and the full UK from 1970.

Sources: 1900-1930 from U.S. Census decennial volumes; 1970-2011 from NYC City Planning; 2020 from the Census ACS 2022 5-year estimates. 1940-1960 are interpolated and labeled as such. Figures between censuses are interpolated and are exact only at each decade. The 1930 city total derived here (2,358,686) matches the published census total.
